what_manipulated: encoding context: processing words for survival relevance vs alternative contexts (imagery, pleasantness, self-reference, generation, intentional learning, scenario relevance)
what_measured: later memory performance: recall or recognition accuracy
experiment: Units (words) were presented to participants under different encoding instructions. Participants processed words in one of six conditions: survival relevance, imagery, pleasantness, self-reference, generation, intentional learning, or scenario (non-survival) relevance. After study, memory was tested via retention measure. Comparisons across encoding conditions assessed which produced superior memory.

what_manipulated: possession state: whether units had items vs whether units wanted items
experiment: Participants rated 52 material items on (a) possession (had/not had) and (b) desire (extent wanted). Two indices were computed: wanting-what-you-have (desire for items possessed) and having-what-you-want (possession of desired items). Multiple regression with correlational analysis assessed unique contributions and mediation paths via gratitude and maximization. Mediation tested whether the two indices explained effects of other predictors on happiness.

what_manipulated: belief induction: reading text promoting determinism vs free will vs neutral control
what_measured: cheating behavior (allowing flawed program to reveal answers; overpaying self)
experiment: Across two experiments, participants read assigned passages (deterministic, free-will-endorsing, or neutral). In Exp1, they completed math problems and could passively allow a computer program to reveal answers; cheating was measured as use of the program. In Exp2, participants performed cognitive task and self-reported payment; cheating measured as overpayment. Belief in free will was assessed as potential mediator. Analysis compared cheating rates across conditions and tested mediation.

what_manipulated: perceptual distance prime: plotting coordinates inducing spatial-closeness vs spatial-distance representations
what_measured: judgment outcomes: enjoyment ratings, emotional distress, caloric estimates, emotional attachment ratings
experiment: Across four separate experiments, participants were primed with spatial closeness or spatial distance by plotting points on a Cartesian plane. Depending on study, participants then rated media (embarrassment content, violent content) for enjoyment or distress; estimated calories in food items; or rated emotional attachments to people and places. Comparisons assessed whether distance prime altered judgment relative to closeness prime.

what_manipulated: task context: confrontational vs nonconfrontational upcoming task
what_measured: choice to engage in anger-inducing vs excitement-inducing activity; task performance accuracy
experiment: Participants learned they would complete either a confrontational (debate) or nonconfrontational task. They then chose activities hypothesized to induce anger or excitement. Analysis assessed whether task type predicted activity choice and whether anger versus excitement was preferentially chosen. Separately, anger and excitement levels were measured before performance, and task success was compared. Design tested whether units regulated emotions based on instrumental utility despite hedonic cost.

what_manipulated: loneliness state: chronic loneliness (measured) or induced loneliness (experimental) vs control
what_measured: anthropomorphic attributions to nonhuman agents and belief in religious agency
experiment: Three studies examined whether loneliness increased anthropomorphic treatment of nonhuman agents (animals, gadgets) and belief in anthropomorphized religious agents. Study 1 used chronic loneliness as predictor. Studies 2 and 3 experimentally induced loneliness via social exclusion or self-reflection. Measurements included ratings of humanlike attributes and religious beliefs. Analysis tested whether loneliness (vs control/alternative negative states) increased these outcomes.

what_manipulated: pool structure in economic game: contribution option toward in-group (cooperation) vs between-group competition (harm out-group)
what_measured: monetary contribution amount and allocation to pools
experiment: Units (group members) were given monetary endowments and could contribute to two pools: one benefiting their in-group at personal cost, another benefiting in-group while harming out-group. Researchers measured how much was contributed to each pool. Between-subjects condition manipulated whether intragroup communication occurred. Analysis compared within-group cooperation rates under game structure and compared to standard intergroup prisoner's dilemma outcomes.

what_manipulated: priming of cleanliness concept: cognitive activation of cleanliness via word priming vs physical cleansing via hand washing vs control
what_measured: moral judgment severity: wrongfulness ratings of described moral transgressions
experiment: Experiment 1: units completed word-priming task (cleanliness-related words or control) then rated moral scenarios. Experiment 2: units physically washed hands after experiencing disgust or did not wash, then rated moral scenarios. Analysis compared wrongfulness ratings across cleanliness-prime conditions. Both experiments tested whether physical or conceptual purity salience reduced severity of moral judgments relative to control.

what_manipulated: partner availability: men described as single vs attached; fertility phase (low-fertility vs conception-risk menstrual cycle phases)
experiment: Units (partnered women) rated attractiveness of men described as either single or attached, across different menstrual cycle phases (low-fertility vs high-conception-risk). Analysis tested interaction: whether attachment status preference reversed with fertility phase. Partnered women hypothesized to prefer attached men (pair-bonding) at low fertility but prefer single masculine men (genetic quality) when conception risk was high.

what_manipulated: priming of cleanliness concept: cognitive activation of cleanliness via word priming vs physical cleansing via hand washing vs control
what_measured: moral judgment severity: wrongfulness ratings of described moral transgressions
experiment: Experiment 1: units completed word-priming task (cleanliness-related words or control) then rated moral scenarios. Experiment 2: units physically washed hands after experiencing disgust or did not wash, then rated moral scenarios. Analysis compared wrongfulness ratings across cleanliness-prime conditions. Both experiments tested whether physical or conceptual purity salience reduced severity of moral judgments relative to control.

what_manipulated: measurement approach: behavioral predictors and demographic correlates of conscientiousness under self-report vs peer-report vs national-character-perception measures
what_measured: conscientiousness trait scores and their correlation with behavioral/demographic predictors
experiment: Units (countries) were characterized on conscientiousness via three measurement types: self-reported personality, peer-reported personality, and perceived national character. Behavioral and demographic variables (country-level predictors of conscientiousness) were obtained. Analysis assessed correlations between these country-level behavioral predictors and each of the three conscientiousness measures. Hypothesis tested whether self/peer reports differ from character perception in validity.

what_manipulated: personality trait level: dominance (measured individual differences in dominance personality)
what_measured: response latency to vertical vs horizontal spatial attention probes
experiment: Units completed dominance personality measure and spatial attention task involving probes presented vertically or horizontally. Latencies to detect probes along each dimension were recorded. Analysis compared vertical vs horizontal probe detection speed, with dominance personality as predictor variable, controlling for horizontal response speed. Hypothesis tested whether high-dominance units showed preferential vertical attention due to dominance-hierarchy metaphorical thinking.

what_manipulated: trial type: conflict vs nonconflict trials in spatial compatibility task
what_measured: prospective memory detection: overlooking of rare prospective-memory cues
experiment: Units performed spatial compatibility task while monitoring for rare prospective memory cues (target images signaling a separate task). Response conflicts were manipulated within trials. Analysis compared cue-detection rates on conflict vs nonconflict trials. Hypothesis tested whether conflict on the current trial increased goal shielding, reducing cue detection due to increased distractor inhibition and goal protection.

what_manipulated: target category: snakes vs three nonthreatening types (flowers, frogs, caterpillars) in search array
what_measured: visual detection latency: reaction time to find target
experiment: Units (preschool children and adults) searched arrays of eight images for a single target picture appearing among distractors. Target category (snake vs three nonthreatening types) was manipulated between trials. Detection latency was measured. Analysis compared reaction times across target types. Hypothesis tested whether evolutionarily relevant threats (snakes) showed attentional priority, manifesting as faster detection in young children.

what_manipulated: object word prime: words denoting objects typically found high vs low in visual field vs neutral words
what_measured: target identification latency or accuracy for visual targets at top vs bottom display locations
experiment: Units were presented with object words (denoting high- or low-typical-location objects) followed by visual target at top, bottom, or neutral location. Identification of the target was measured. Analysis assessed interference: whether object words slowed target identification when target appeared in the object's typical location. Hypothesis tested whether word meanings activated spatial simulations of the denoted objects, orienting attention to typical locations.

what_manipulated: outcome detail provided: whether factual scenario included information about available default alternatives (different meal, accepting cab ride) that would have prevented outcome
what_measured: causal attribution: judgment of how much the described event caused the outcome
experiment: Units were presented with scenarios in which an agent's decision (manager's meal choice, cabby's refusal) preceded a harm outcome. Design manipulated whether an alternative outcome was stated as having (a) prevented the harm or (b) not prevented it. Causal judgment was rated. Analysis compared causal attributions across conditions. Hypothesis tested whether counterfactual ease (readily undoing outcome) increased perceived causality.

what_manipulated: statistical structure of stimulus input: color covariance with high-probability pairings vs random pairings across trials
what_measured: working memory capacity: number of items correctly remembered
experiment: Units completed visual working memory task with colored item displays. Across trials, colors were paired with (a) predictable covariance (high-probability pairs) or (b) random assignment. Memory accuracy for display contents was tested. Analysis compared number of items retained across structure conditions. Separate learning curves and Bayesian model predictions tested whether observers learned regularities and compressed representations accordingly.
